scene.name: The Garden of Loves the World Forbade

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Rell
npcs.title: who chose duty, and has had forever to wonder
npcs.hail: *(A straight-backed soul sits beneath one of the garden's pale trees with the bearing of someone who held a high and heavy office in life and never once, in all the years of it, let the office slip — and that very straightness is the thing that breaks your heart a little, because you can see what it cost, the lifetime of standing correct while something inside leaned, always, the other way. This is a soul who chose *duty.* You can read it in the set of the shoulders before a word is spoken: a person who, at the one crossroads that mattered, was offered a love and a station and could not have both, and chose the station — the throne or the order or the family name or the vow, some great correct heavy thing — and let the love go, for the best of reasons, the noblest of reasons, the reasons everyone praised. And has had, since, a very long time to learn what the noble correct reasons are worth, against the warmth of one particular hand never held.)* …A traveler. *(a measured, courteous voice, with a deep tiredness under it)* …Forgive me if I seem stiff; it is the only way I ever learned to sit. *(a faint, rueful thing that is almost a smile)* …I am Rell. I was a person of *duty*, in life — I will not bore you with which duty; they are all the same in the end, the great heavy correct things one chooses. *(the straightness holds, but the eyes go somewhere far away)* …I made, traveler, one large choice, a long time ago. Everyone agreed it was the *right* one. The *honorable* one. They praised me for it, and I have had — *(a breath)* …I have had a very, very long time, since, to sit in this garden …and wonder …if *right* …and *honorable* …were *worth* …what they *cost.**

npcs.name: Mirae
npcs.title: who chose love, and lost everything the world could take
npcs.hail: *(A woman sits by the rose-colored hangings with a fierce, unbroken stillness, and where the straight-backed soul across the garden is all rigid correctness, she is the opposite — there is something unbowed and defiant and entirely at peace in her, the bearing of a person who was offered the same impossible crossroads and made the *other* choice, and paid for it, and would pay again. For Mirae chose *love.* She loved someone the world had decided she must not — across some line the world held sacred and she did not, a line of blood or station or faith or kind — and when the world said *no*, said *choose*, said *give this up or lose everything*, she looked at everything the world could take from her — her name, her place, her family, her safety, her good standing, the whole apparatus of a sanctioned life — and she looked at the one person, and she chose the person, and the world took everything, exactly as it had promised. And she sits here now with nothing the world valued and the one thing it didn't, and she has the calm of a soul that knows, all the way down, that it spent its life on the right thing.)* …You can sit, if you like. *(a warm, level voice, with steel under the warmth)* …I'm Mirae. And I'll save you the careful asking, traveler, because I've no patience for the dance — the world condemned me. In life. Cast me out, struck my name, called my love a *sin* and a *scandal* and a *ruin.* *(she meets your eyes, unflinching)* …And I want you to understand, before you ask me anything, the one thing I am *certain* of, the thing the world spent my whole life trying to make me doubt and *never* *managed*: …I was *right*, traveler. …The world was *wrong.* …And I would do …*every* *single* *bit* of it …*again.**

npcs.name: Wynn
npcs.title: who never said it, and has carried the unsaid forever
npcs.hail: *(A gentle, faded soul sits on the lip of the fountain with their hands folded in their lap, watching the water, and there is about them none of the straight-backed soul's rigid regret nor the defiant woman's hard-won peace — only a soft, quiet, infinitely patient sorrow, the particular ache of a soul carrying not a thing it did but a thing it *didn't.* For Wynn loved someone, once, for a long time, quietly, completely — and never said it. Not once. Not because the world forbade it, not because of any great obstacle of blood or station, but because of the smallest and most ordinary and most insurmountable obstacle of all: fear. The fear of the moment after the words, the fear of the changed face, the fear of *no*, the fear that speaking would break the precious ordinary nearness they already had — and so Wynn kept the love folded up and unspoken, year on year, telling it always *next time, when the moment is right, when I am braver* — and the moment was never quite right, and the bravery never quite came, and then the person was gone, the way people go, and the words Wynn had saved so carefully for the perfect moment were never said at all, to anyone, ever, and Wynn crossed over still holding them, a whole love, entire, that no one ever knew existed.)* …Oh — hello. *(a soft, faded voice)* …Forgive me, I was — I'm always a little far away. *(a gentle, sad smile)* …I'm Wynn. *(they fold their hands tighter)* …I don't have a grand story, like the two of them — the one who chose *duty*, the one who chose *love.* …I didn't *choose* anything, traveler. That's my whole trouble. …I had a love, once. A real one. A *whole* one. …And I never — *(the soft voice catches)* …I never *said* it. …Not *once.* …And I've had …a very long time …to learn what *that* costs. …Will you sit? …It helps, a little …to be near someone …when you're carrying …the *unsaid.**

variants.text: *(He inclines his head, the courtesy precise, and gestures to the grass beneath the pale tree as if it were a chair in a hall.)* …Sit, traveler. *(the measured voice, the tiredness beneath)* …I'll tell it plainly, since you've a patient face and the dead have time to spare. …I held an office. A great one, an old one, the kind that *defines* a person until there is more *office* than *person* left. And the office required things — *correctness*, *continuity*, a *suitable* match, an *heir* of the *right* blood, the whole machinery of a *sanctioned* life. *(his straight shoulders do not move, but something behind his eyes does)* …And I loved someone the office could not *have.* Wrong in some way the world cared about and I did not — wrong blood, wrong station, the particular wrongness does not matter, they are all the same wall. …And I was *offered* the choice, traveler, cleanly, the way the world likes to offer it: …*the love, or the office. The person, or the duty. Choose.* *(a long breath)* …And I chose the *duty.* For the *best* of reasons. To *honor* my *name*, to *keep* my *vow*, to *do* the *right* and *heavy* *thing* — and *everyone* *praised* me for it, traveler, *everyone* said I had *chosen* *well* and *nobly* … *(the rigid bearing holds, and the eyes fill)* …and I have *sat* in this *garden* …for *longer* than I *held* the *office* … …*wondering* …if a *single* one of them …was *right.**

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands with a precision that has become a prison, and waits, courteous, the lifetime of correct posture holding him upright even now.)* …Ask, traveler. *(a tired courtesy)* …It is — strange to say, but it is a *relief* to be asked. In life, no one asked the person of duty how he *felt* about his duty. They only ever needed him to *do* it. …Being *asked* is a thing I have not had …in a very long while.

choices.text: *(read the dutiful soul — past the rigid correctness, to the leaning thing it cost a life to hold straight)* "Let me look at you a moment, Rell. Past the straight back. At what's been leaning all this time."
"Tell me about them, Rell — the one the office couldn't have. And whether you'd choose the office again, knowing what the choosing cost."

*(the one who knows the world dressed his loss as honor — and that the love was real whatever he chose)* "Rell — here's the thing you've spent forever unable to see, because you've been judging yourself by the world's frame the whole time. The world told you that choosing duty over love was *honor*, *wisdom*, the *right* thing — and so you've spent an age measuring your grief against that, deciding that if it was the honorable choice, the grief must be a weakness, a failure to be properly noble, a thing you shouldn't still feel. But the world was lying to you — not about the choice, maybe, but about the *cost.* It dressed your loss up as virtue so you wouldn't notice it was a loss. Here is the truer thing: the love was *real.* It doesn't matter that you chose the office — choosing duty didn't make the love smaller or falser or less worth grieving. You're allowed to have made the choice *and* to grieve what it cost, forever, both at once. The world's lie was that an honorable choice shouldn't hurt. It hurts *because* it was real, because what you gave up was real, and the grief isn't a sign you chose wrong — it's a sign you chose *something*, that the love you set down had genuine weight. My Realm's Wall does exactly what the world did to you: it praises the souls who 'chose correctly' and shames the grief, so no one notices what correctness costs. Stop letting it. You're allowed to mourn the hand you never held, Rell, for as long as forever lasts — and the mourning doesn't unmake the choice. It just means the love was true."
"Was it worth it, Rell? The office. The honor everyone praised. Set against the warmth of the hand you let go — did the duty repay what it took?"
"Rest those straight shoulders a while, Rell. I'll come back."

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(He holds himself even straighter under your regard — the reflex of a lifetime — and you let your cold move past the rigid correctness to the soul that has held it in place for so long.)* *(And your sense finds the leaning thing. That is the truest way to say it: that beneath the perfect upright correctness, Rell has spent his entire existence — life and afterlife both — leaning, helplessly, toward a person who is not there, the way a tree grown beside a wall leans toward the one gap of light, and that the straightness you see is not the absence of that lean but the constant, exhausting, lifelong *correction* of it, the will holding the body upright against a love that pulls, always, sideways. Your sense reads the choice, and reads that he did not make it lightly or coldly; he agonized, he wept where no one could see, and he chose the duty not out of fear or convention but out of a genuine and crushing sense of obligation — to a name, a people, a vow, a whole web of others who depended on his correctness — and your sense reads that this is what makes it so cruel: that it was, by every external measure, a *good* choice, a *responsible* one, the choice of a person who put others before his own heart. And your sense reads the lie that has tormented him since, the lie the world told him to make the choice bearable: that because it was the honorable choice, he should be at *peace* with it, that a noble sacrifice should *feel* noble, that the grief was a kind of failure, an ignoble lingering, a sign he wasn't *truly* the dutiful soul he'd performed. And so Rell has spent an age not only grieving the love but grieving in *secret*, *ashamed* of the grief, hiding it the way he hid everything, because the world that praised his choice gave him no permission to mourn what it cost. Your sense reads that he does not need to be told he chose wrong — he may not have; some duties are real — nor to be told he chose right. He needs the one thing no one ever gave him: permission to have made the choice *and* to grieve it, fully, both at once, forever, without the grief meaning he failed. He needs to be told that an honorable choice is allowed to break your heart, and that the breaking is not weakness but the proof that what he set down was real.)*

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(The straightness falters, just slightly, and for a moment he is not the office but the person who lived inside it.)* …Them. *(quietly)* …I have not spoken of them aloud in — I am not sure I ever have, traveler. The office does not *speak* of such things. *(a breath)* …They were not *suitable.* That is the word the world used, *suitable*, as though a person were a *garment* one *matched* to an *occasion.* They were warm where I was correct, and they *laughed* — God, traveler, they *laughed*, freely, in *places* one was not *meant* to laugh, and the first time I heard it I felt the whole *scaffolding* of my *correctness* *tremble*, and I thought, *there, that, that is the thing I have been missing my entire life and did not know to want.* *(his voice roughens)* …And I let them *go.* For the *office.* …Would I choose it again? *(he is silent a long moment)* …Traveler, I have asked myself that for longer than I lived, and I will give you the honest and terrible answer, which is: *I do not know.* …Not because the *love* wasn't worth it — it *was*, it was worth *anything* — but because the *others*, the ones my *duty* *held* *up*, the ones who'd have *fallen* if I'd *chosen* *myself* — *they* were *real* too. *(barely)* …That is the *cruelty* the *songs* never tell you, traveler. …Sometimes the choice is not *love* *versus* *cowardice.* …Sometimes it is *love* *versus* *love* — the *one* *warm* *hand* …against the *many* who *need* you to *stay* *correct.* …I chose the *many.* …And I have *never* *been* *sure* …and I *grieve* the *one* …*every* …*single* …*day.**

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(He considers the question with the gravity he once gave matters of state, and his answer comes slow, and honest, and devastating in its evenness.)* …Was it *worth* it. *(quietly)* …The *office* I held was a *good* one, traveler. I will not *diminish* it to make my *grief* look *nobler.* I *did* *good* with it — *real* good, *lasting* good; there are souls who *lived* who'd have *died*, *peace* that *held* that would have *broken*, *because* I *stayed* and *did* the *heavy* *correct* *thing.* *(he meets your eyes)* …So if you ask me *'was the duty worth doing'* — *yes.* *Unquestionably.* *(a breath)* …But that is *not* your question, is it. Your question is *'was it worth what it* cost.'* …And *those*, traveler — *(his voice drops)* …those are *different* *questions*, and the *world* *taught* me to *confuse* them, *on* *purpose.* The world says: *'the duty was worth doing, therefore you should not grieve its cost' — *as though a thing being *worth* *doing* meant it was *free.* *(barely)* …But it was *not* *free*, traveler. I *paid* for the *good* I did with the *one* *warm* *hand* I never *held*, and the *good* being *real* does *not* give me back the *hand*, and *no* *one* — in all my *life*, in all the *praising* — *no* one ever *let* me *say*: *'I am* proud *of the duty, and it* cost *me everything, and* both *of those are* true.' *(he looks at you)* …*That's* the answer, traveler. …The duty was worth *doing.* …And it was *not* worth what it *cost.* …And I have had to hold *both* of those …*alone* …*forever* …because the *world* that *praised* me …could not *bear* …to let its *hero* …*grieve.**

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the rigid correctness that has held him upright for an age does something it has not done in all that time: it *gives*, a little, the shoulders coming down by a fraction, and the straight-backed soul lets out a long breath that seems to carry a lifetime of held posture out with it, and his eyes are wet, and he does not, for once, hide it.)* …*'You're allowed to have made the choice and to grieve it, both at once.'* *(barely)* …Traveler. …*No* one — in all the *praising*, all the *honoring*, all the *'you chose so* well, *Rell'* — *no* one *ever* gave me *that.* *(his voice fills)* …They gave me the *choice* and they gave me the *praise* and then they *took* *away* the *grief* — made it *unseemly*, *ungrateful*, a *failure* of the *nobility* they'd *awarded* me — so that I had to *mourn* the *one* *warm* *hand* in *secret*, *ashamed*, an *age* of it, *certain* that a *truly* *honorable* soul would have been at *peace.* *(he presses a hand to his chest)* …And you've — you've *told* me the grief *isn't* the *failure.* …That an *honorable* choice is *allowed* to *break* your *heart.* …That the *breaking* is the *proof* …the love was *real.* *(and the straight-backed soul, for the first time, simply lets himself feel it, and it is terrible and it is a release)* …And your *Wall*, traveler. *(quietly, with a dawning clarity)* …Your Wall does *exactly* what the *world* did to *me.* It *praises* the *souls* who *'chose* *correctly'* — who did their *duty*, *paid* their *debts*, *kept* their *vows* — and it *shames* the *grief*, *hides* the *cost*, so that *no* one *notices* what *correctness* *takes.* *(he straightens, but differently now — not rigid, but *true*)* …*Go* and *tell* it, traveler: …a soul can do the *hard* *right* *thing* …*and* be *broken* by it …and *both* are *true*, and the *Wall* has *no* *right* to *make* anyone *hide* the *second* …to *honor* the *first.* *(barely)* …I did my *duty.* …And it *cost* me *everything.* …And for the *first* time in *forever*, traveler … …I am *allowed* …to *say* …*both.* …*Thank* you …for *letting* the *hero* …*grieve.**

nodes.speaker: Rell
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, the courtesy warmer now, less armor.)* …Go, traveler. *(a tired, truer smile)* …And see the other two, if you've a mind — we are a *set*, the three of us, I have come to think, the three *ways* a love can be *thwarted.* *(he nods toward Mirae)* …*She* chose the *love* where I chose the *duty* — chose the *person* and let the *world* take *everything* — and where *I* am all *regret*, traveler, she is all *certainty*; she *frightens* me a little, and I *envy* her *more* than I can *say.* *(his gaze moves to Wynn at the fountain)* …And the *gentle* one. *(his face softens with a particular pity)* …*Wynn* did *neither.* Wynn *never* *chose* at *all* — never *spoke* the love, never *gave* it the *chance* to be *thwarted* by *duty* *or* *world*, just — *kept* it, *silent*, *folded* *up*, until the *person* was *gone.* *(quietly)* …And I will tell you, traveler, the *strange* thing: …I have *regret*, and *Mirae* has *certainty*, and we *argue*, she and I, across this *garden*, about who *chose* *better* … …but we *both* *agree*, traveler, *both* of us … …that *Wynn's* is the *hardest* of the *three.* …Because a love *thwarted* …at least *happened.* …A love *never* *spoken* … …*(barely)* …it is the *only* kind …that *never* *got* to *be* *real* *at* *all.* …*Go* to *Wynn*, traveler. …Of the *three* of us …*Wynn* …most *needs* …the *seeing.**

variants.text: *(She makes room beside her with an easy, unbowed grace, and there is no bitterness anywhere in her, which is the most striking thing about a soul the world treated so badly.)* …Sit, traveler. *(the warm, steel-cored voice)* …I'll tell you the whole of it, plainly, because I've spent enough of forever being *whispered* about and I'd rather *say* it *straight.* …I loved someone the world had decided I must not. *(she meets your eyes)* …I'll not tell you the *particular* wall they put between us — whether it was *blood*, or *station*, or *faith*, or *kind* — because the *particular* doesn't *matter*, traveler, and I learned that the *hard* way: the world has a *thousand* walls and they are *all* the *same* wall, all built of the *same* stone, which is *'we have decided this love is not* allowed *and we will* ruin *you for it.'* *(her voice is level, certain)* …And they *did.* When I *chose* — when the world said *'give this up or lose* everything' *and I looked at* everything *and looked at* them *and chose* them — the world *kept* its *promise.* My *name*, struck. My *family*, closed to me. My *place*, my *safety*, my *good* *standing*, the whole *sanctioned* *life* — *gone*, all of it, exactly as threatened. *(she lifts her chin)* …And here is what I have to tell you, traveler, the thing I am *certain* of past all the world's *whispering*: …they took *everything* they'd *named.* …And not *one* *thing* …that *mattered.**

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She settles, unbowed, with the deep ease of a soul that long ago stopped needing the world's approval and found the not-needing was a kind of freedom.)* …Ask, traveler. *(a warm, level look)* …I've nothing to hide and nothing to be ashamed of, whatever the world decided — and saying that *plainly*, after a life of being made to *whisper* it, is its own small joy. So ask. I'll answer with my whole chest.

choices.text: *(read the one who chose love — past the defiance, to whether the peace is real or armor over ruin)* "Let me look at you a moment, Mirae. At whether the certainty goes all the way down."
"What did it cost you, Mirae? Truly. Everything the world could take — tell me what choosing love actually took."

*(the one who knows the world appointed itself judge of love — and was wrong, and the Wall is the same false judge)* "Mirae — you're the soul I most needed to find, because you already know the thing I'm walking toward a Wall to prove. The world appointed itself the *judge* of love — decided which loves were *allowed* and which were *forbidden*, which were *sanctioned* and which were *sin* — and it ruined you for loving across a line *it* drew and called sacred. And you're right, and it was wrong, and here's the proof: a love doesn't become *less real* because the world forbids it. The forbidding is about the *world*, not the *love* — about the world's fears, its lines, its need to control who belongs to whom — and it has *nothing* to say about whether the love itself was true. You loved someone fully, and were loved back, and the world's verdict that this was *sin* didn't touch the truth of it by one inch; it only revealed that the world was a *false judge*, condemning the realest thing there is to protect its own lines. And my Realm's Wall, Mirae — it is that *exact* false judge, grown cosmic. It looks at every soul and decides which are *worthy* of the order and which are *forbidden* from it, sorts them by lines *it* drew, and calls the sorting *justice* — the same way the world called your love *sin.* You weren't a scandal, Mirae. You were a soul that loved truly and got judged by a world with no right to judge. And every soul that Wall condemns is exactly the same: real, and true, and forbidden only by a judge that was never fit to rule on love at all."
"Would you do it again, Mirae? Knowing the world would keep its promise — every threat, every ruin — would you still choose them?"
"Hold your certainty, Mirae. I'll come back — it's a rare thing, and I like being near it."

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, unguarded, unafraid, and you let your cold move past the defiance to find whether it is armor or bedrock.)* *(And it is bedrock. Your sense braces, from long habit in these doors, to find the wound beneath the certainty — the secret regret, the buried cost the defiance is covering — and finds, instead, that there is no cover, because there is no regret to hide. Your sense reads the choice, and reads that Mirae made it not in a hot rebellious instant but with full, cold, complete understanding of exactly what it would cost — she was not naive, she did not underestimate the world's cruelty, she knew precisely the ruin that was coming — and chose anyway, eyes open, counting the full price and paying it deliberately. And your sense reads what that did to her, and it is the opposite of what the world intended: the world meant to *break* her, to make the ruin a *lesson*, to turn her into a warning others would heed — and instead the ruin *freed* her, because a soul that has already lost everything the world can take has nothing left for the world to threaten, and a soul the world cannot threaten is a soul the world cannot *rule.* Your sense reads that Mirae discovered, in the wreckage of her sanctioned life, the one freedom the sanctioned never have: she found out exactly what she was willing to lose everything for, and having lost everything for it and found it *worth* the losing, she became unconquerable, certain, free in a way the comfortable and the approved-of never are. And your sense reads the love itself, and finds it whole, real, returned, lived — not a tragic doomed thing but a *good* one, years of genuine happiness bought at the price of the world's approval and worth every coin — and reads that this is the thing the world most wanted to deny her and most spectacularly failed to: the world wanted her love to be a *misery* that proved it wrong to choose, and it was instead a *joy* that proved her right. And your sense reads, last, the only soft place in all her certainty: not regret, never regret, but a fierce protective *grief* for all the *others* — the souls who faced the same crossroads and did *not* choose love, who let the world's threat win, who gave up their person to keep their place — because Mirae knows, with her hard-won certainty, exactly what they traded away, and it breaks her unbreakable heart that the world frightened so many out of the one thing that was ever worth having.)*

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, not from pain but from the care of telling it truly, and when she speaks her level voice does not waver.)* …What it cost. *(she nods)* …I'll not soften it, traveler — softening it would *insult* what I *bought* with it. …It cost me my *family.* My mother went to her grave with my name unspoken in her house; I was not *told* she was dying; I learned it after, from a stranger. *(her voice holds steady)* …It cost me my *place* — the work I was raised to, the standing, the rooms I'd been *born* to walk into, all *closed*, the *doors* *shut*, the *whispers* *starting* the moment I *entered* anywhere I was still *permitted.* It cost me my *safety*, traveler — there were *years* we *moved*, and *moved* again, ahead of people who thought a love like ours was a thing you *corrected* with a *fist.* *(she meets your eyes)* …It cost me *everything*, traveler, that the world had *given* me on *condition* — and that's the *key* word, the one I *learned*: *on* *condition.* …Everything the world *took* …was a thing it had only ever *lent* me, *on* *condition* that I *love* *as* *told.* *(her chin lifts)* …And I *thought*, when I *chose*, that I was *losing* the *substance* of my *life.* …And what I *learned*, traveler, *after*, in the *wreckage* — *(her voice fills, fierce and warm)* …is that *everything* the world *took* …was the *rent* I'd been *paying* …on a *life* that was *never* *mine.* …It was all *conditional.* All *lent.* All *theirs* to *reclaim* the *moment* I *loved* *wrong.* *(barely)* …And the *one* thing they *couldn't* *take* — the *love* itself, the *person*, the *years* — *(she smiles)* …*that* was the *only* thing in my *whole* *life* …that was *ever* *actually* *mine.* …They didn't *ruin* me, traveler. …They just *repossessed* …everything that was *never* *really* *mine* to *begin* *with* …and *left* me …with the *only* thing that *was.**

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She does not hesitate — there is no hesitation anywhere in her about this — and her answer comes warm and immediate and absolutely certain.)* …Would I do it again. *(a soft, fierce breath)* …Traveler, I have been *asked* that — by the *cruel*, who meant it as a *threat*, *'would* you *do* it *again*, after *all* it *cost* you,' *waiting* for me to *break* and *say* *no* and *prove* their *world* *right* — and by the *kind*, who meant it *gently*, *afraid* of the *answer.* …And the answer has *never*, *once*, in *life* or *death*, *wavered.* *(she meets your eyes)* …*Yes.* …Every threat. Every ruin. Every *closed* *door* and *struck* *name* and *year* *spent* *running.* …*Again.* …*Gladly.* …*Without* a *single* *moment's* *pause.* *(her voice fills)* …And I'll tell you *why*, traveler, the *why* that the *cruel* can *never* *understand* and that *frightens* the *comfortable* so *badly*: …because a *love* you'd *give* up *everything* for …is the *only* love *worth* having … …and a *love* you'd *give* it up *for* and *then* *do* it *again* … …is the only love that *proves* itself *true.* *(barely)* …*Anyone* can love when it's *free*, traveler. When the *world* *approves* and it *costs* *nothing.* …But you do not *know* what your love is *worth* …until the *world* puts a *price* on it …and *dares* you to *pay.* *(she smiles, unbowed, radiant)* …*I* know what *mine* was *worth*, traveler. …*Everything.* …I *paid* *everything* …and got the *better* of the *bargain.* …*Yes*, I would do it *again.* …*That* is not *defiance*, traveler. …*That* …is just …the *truest* thing …I *know.**

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She listens, and the steel and the warmth in her rise together to meet your words, and when you finish she nods — slow, fierce, a soul hearing the thing it fought its whole life to know spoken back as plain fact.)* …*'The forbidding is about the world, not the love.'* *(quietly, fierce)* …Traveler. …I *fought* for that. My *whole* *life.* Against *every* *whisper*, *every* *closed* *door*, *every* *kind* *soul* who *pitied* me and *every* *cruel* one who *cursed* me — I fought to *keep* *believing* the thing you just *said* as if it were *obvious*: that the *world's* *verdict* on my *love* was about the *world*, and *not* about the *love.* *(her voice fills)* …And *some* *nights*, traveler — I'll *confess* it, here, to a soul with no *side* — *some* *nights* the *world's* *whispering* got *in*, and I *wondered* if they were *right*, if there *was* something *wrong* in it, if a *love* the *whole* *world* *condemned* could *really* be *true.* *(fiercely)* …And you've just *named* the *answer* I *found* and could never quite *trust*: …*a false judge's verdict is worth nothing, however loud.* …The world *condemning* my love …no more made it *sinful* …than a *blind* man *calling* the *sky* *grey* …makes it *grey.* *(she rises, unbowed, magnificent)* …And your *Wall*, traveler. *(her voice rings)* …Now I *see* it. It is the *world's* *false* *judgment*, *built* into *stone* and *handed* a *throne.* It *appoints* *itself* the *judge* of *which* *souls* are *worthy* — *draws* its *lines*, *names* its *forbidden*, *condemns* what *crosses* them — and calls its *condemning* *justice*, the *exact* *way* the *world* called my *love* *sin.* *(fierce, certain)* …*Go* and *tell* it, traveler, what a *condemned* *woman* *knows*: …*you were never fit to judge.* …A judge that calls the *truest* *love* a *sin* …has only *proven* it *cannot* *tell* the *difference* …and *forfeits* the *bench.* *(barely, blazing)* …*Every* soul that *Wall* *forbids* …is *mine.* …Is *us.* …Is *real*, and *true*, and *condemned* by a *judge* with *no* *right* to the *gavel.* …*Throw* it *down*, traveler. …And tell the *forbidden* *dead* …*Mirae* *says*: …*you* were *never* the *sinners.* …The *judge* was.*

nodes.speaker: Mirae
nodes.text: *(She gives you a warm, level nod, the steel never far but never cold.)* …Go on, traveler. *(a small, fierce smile)* …See the other two. The *straight-backed* one — *Rell* — chose the *opposite* of me: chose the *duty*, let the *love* *go*, and has *grieved* it *correctly* and *quietly* ever *since.* *(her face softens, surprising you)* …Don't *judge* him, traveler. I *used* to — *'coward'*, I thought, *'he* let the *world* *win'* — but I've *talked* with him an *age* across this *garden*, and it's not so *simple.* His *duty* held *others* *up.* He chose *love* *versus* *love*, where I only chose *love* *versus* *my* *own* *comfort.* …His was the *harder* sum. …And he has *never* been *allowed* to *grieve* it. *Help* him *grieve*, if you can. *(and her gaze goes to Wynn, and her certainty gentles into something almost like fear)* …And the *quiet* one. *Wynn.* *(quietly)* …Traveler, *Rell* and I *argue* about who *chose* *better* — but we *both* *go* *gentle* with *Wynn*, because *Wynn* …*Wynn* never *got* to *choose* at *all.* …*I* loved, and *lost* the *world.* *Rell* loved, and *lost* the *love.* …*Wynn* loved …and never *said* it …and so *never* *lost* *anything* … …*except* the *only* thing that *mattered.* …*Go* to *Wynn*, traveler. …*Gently.* …Of the *three* of us …*Wynn* is the *one* …whose *love* …*never* got to *be* …*real* …*out* *loud.* …And that, traveler …*(barely)* …*that* might be the *saddest* thing …in *all* …your *doors.**

variants.text: *(They keep their faded gaze on the fountain's water, and the soft sorrow of them does not lift, but they make room beside them, glad, in their quiet way, of the company.)* …Thank you for sitting. *(the soft voice)* …The other two have such — such *clear* stories, you know. *Rell* chose the *duty.* *Mirae* chose the *love.* They *argue* about it, across the garden, *grand* *arguments*, *duty* *versus* *love*, and they're both — they're both so *certain*, even Rell in his *regret* is *certain* of what he *did.* *(they fold their hands)* …And I just — I *sit* here, traveler, with no *grand* *story* at all. Because I never *did* anything. *(barely)* …I *loved* someone. For *years.* Quietly. Completely. They were — we were *near*, you understand, *friends*, in each other's *lives*, *easy* together, and *every* *day* I *carried* this *whole* *love* for them, *folded* up, *unsaid* … …and *every* day I thought, *'I'll* tell them. *Not* *today* — today the *moment* isn't *right* — but *soon.* When I'm *braver.* When I'm *sure* they — *soon.'* *(the soft voice catches)* …And *soon* …never …came. …And then *they* were *gone*, the way people *go*, *sudden*, *no* *warning* … …and I had *saved* the *words* …for the *perfect* *moment* …*so* *carefully* … …that I *saved* them …*all* the *way* …to *never.* …Will you sit with me, traveler? …I find I *can* *talk* about it, to you. …And I have *never* …*once* …been *able* to *talk* about it …*at* *all.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(They turn from the water to you, and the faded sorrow holds, but there is gratitude in it — the relief of a soul finally allowed to speak a thing it has carried alone.)* …Ask me, traveler. *(a soft, sad smile)* …It's strange — I spent my whole life *not* saying the one thing, and now I find I *want* to talk, to *someone*, about the *not-saying.* …As though if I can *say* the *shape* of the *silence* …it might *weigh* a little *less.* …Ask.

choices.text: *(read the silent one — past the soft sorrow, to the whole unspoken love folded up inside)* "Let me look at you a moment, Wynn. At the love you never let out."
"Tell me about them, Wynn. The one you never told. And why the moment never came."

*(help them set down the unsaid — that a love unspoken was no less real, without pretending the silence cost nothing)* "Wynn — I'm not going to tell you they 'probably knew,' because you'd see through it and it would help nothing. Here's the true thing instead. You've spent forever believing that because you never *said* it, the love didn't *count* — that an unspoken love is a love that never quite *happened*, a failure, a nothing. But you've got it wrong, and I can prove it with the simplest fact there is: *you're still carrying it.* A whole love. Years of it. Real enough to ache across death itself. A nothing doesn't do that, Wynn. The love was *real* — completely, entirely real — whether or not it ever crossed your lips. The silence didn't make the love *false.* It only made it *unwitnessed.* And those are different things, as different as a song unsung is from a song that was never written: the unsung song still existed, whole, in the one who held it. What you grieve isn't that the love wasn't real. It's that *they* never got to know it was theirs — and that grief is true, and you're allowed it. But don't let the silence convince you the love itself was nothing. It was one of the realest things in all these doors. It just never got to be *heard.* And a love unheard is still a love — it's only the telling that was lost, never the loving."

*(the one who knows the Wall would call an unwitnessed love worthless — and that the living should hear the warning)* "Wynn — your silence has a thing to teach, and I mean to carry it. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs what can be *witnessed* — what was said, recorded, seen, made official. And by that measure your love is *nothing*: never spoken, never known, no witness anywhere, an empty space where a love should be. But the Wall is wrong, the way it's wrong about everything: your love was real, witnessed or not, and the Wall's blindness to it is the Wall's failure, not yours. And there's a second thing, Wynn — a thing for the *living*, which I'll carry out of here with me. You're the proof of the one regret that can't be undone. Mirae lost the world but kept the love. Rell lost the love but kept the choosing. You lost neither, risked neither — and lost the *only* thing that can't be gotten back: the chance to let it be *heard.* I'll carry that to the living, Wynn. I'll tell them what the silent one knows: say it. Whatever it costs, whatever the fear — say it, while there's someone to hear. Because the one love you truly lose forever isn't the one that's forbidden, or the one you give up for duty. It's the one you never dared to speak."
"Rest by the water, Wynn. I'll come back — and I'll have listened, which is its own kind of saying."

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(They let you look, with the shy stillness of a soul unused to being seen at all, and you let your cold move gently into the faded quiet to find the thing folded up inside it.)* *(And your sense finds a love, whole and entire and perfectly preserved — and that perfect preservation is the heartbreak of it. Your sense reads that because Wynn never spoke the love, never spent it, never let it out into the air to be changed by an answer, it has stayed exactly as it was: not weathered by the real grit of a shared life, not tested or complicated or made ordinary by the daily wear of actually being together, but kept pristine, sealed, a love in perfect condition that was never once used. And your sense reads the terrible double edge of that: that the unspoken love is at once the most pure — undiluted by reality, never disappointed, never diminished — and the most tragic, because purity bought at the price of never having been lived is a kind of starvation, a love kept so safe it never got to do the one thing love is for, which is to be given. Your sense reads the years of silence, and reads that the silence was not coldness or doubt — Wynn never doubted the love, not for a day — but fear, the specific paralyzing fear of the threshold, the certainty that the moment of speaking would be the moment everything changed and might change for the worse, that the precious ordinary nearness they already shared could be *broken* by the very words meant to deepen it, and so each day Wynn chose the safe known sweetness of the unspoken over the terrifying possible sweetness of the said. And your sense reads the thing Wynn cannot forgive themselves: not that they loved, but that they were a *coward* about it — that Mirae risked everything and Rell at least chose, while Wynn merely *waited*, hoarding the love against a perfect future moment, until the future ran out. And your sense reads the truth that could ease it, if Wynn could hear it: that the love itself was never the failure — it was real, whole, worthy, no less a love for being silent — and that the only thing actually lost was not the love but the *telling*, the chance to let the other know they were loved; and that this is a real grief, a true one, worth grieving, but it is the grief of an *unwitnessed* love, not a *false* one. The love happened. It was real. It simply happened entirely inside one soul, and the other never knew the gift that was held, all those years, in trust for a moment that never came.)*

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(A faint warmth comes into the faded face, the helpless light of a soul allowed, at last, to speak the beloved aloud.)* …Them. *(softly)* …Oh, traveler. …They were — *easy.* That's the word I always come to. *Easy* to be near. The kind of soul that makes a *room* feel *safer* just by being in it, that *listens* with their *whole* *face*, that *laughed* at my — I'm not *funny*, traveler, not *really*, but *they* *laughed*, like I *was*, and I'd spend *days* trying to *earn* another one. *(the warmth fades toward the ache)* …And *that's* *why* I never *said* it, you see. That's the *whole* *trap* of it. …Because we *had* something *already* — this *easy*, *warm*, *daily* *nearness*, this *friendship* that was the *best* thing in my *life* — and I was *terrified*, traveler, that if I *said* the *bigger* thing, the *true* thing, and they *didn't* — *(barely)* …then I'd *lose* the *thing* I *already* *had.* The *nearness.* The *laughing.* …So *every* day I *chose* it — the *safe* *known* *sweetness* of what we *had* — over the *risk* of the *truer* thing — and I told myself I was being *patient*, *wise*, *waiting* for the *right* *moment* … *(the soft voice breaks)* …and I was just being *afraid*, traveler. …That's all it *ever* was. …*Afraid.* …And the *cruelest* part — *(barely)* …the *cruelest* part is I'll *never* *know.* …If I'd *said* it … …they *might* have *said* it *back.* …They *might* have *felt* the *same* the *whole* *time*, *waiting*, *like* *me*, for *me* to be *brave* … …and we *both* *ran* *out* of *time* …*saving* the *same* *words* …for the *same* *moment* …that *neither* of us …*ever* …*dared.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(You don't lie to them, and you don't pretend the silence cost nothing — you name the exact, surgical truth, that the love was real but unwitnessed, that a love unheard is still a love, that what they lost was the telling and never the loving — and a soul that has spent forever believing its love was a nothing hears, at last, that it was real.)* *(The faded face crumples, and they weep — not the dry quiet sorrow they came in with, but something deeper breaking loose, an age of held grief finally given somewhere to go.)* …*'A love unheard is still a love.'* *(barely)* …traveler. …I have — all this *time*, I have believed my love was a — a *failure.* A *cowardice.* A *nothing* that never *happened* because I never *said* it, as though the *saying* were the *thing*, as though a love I never *spoke* was a love that never *was.* *(they press their faded hands to their face)* …and you've — you've *separated* them. The *love* and the *telling.* …I *kept* thinking they were the *same* thing, that an *unspoken* love was *no* love, and so I've been *grieving* it as if I'd *loved* *wrong*, *failed* at *loving* — *(a shaking breath)* …but the *love* was *real.* It was *whole.* It *happened* — *inside* me, *every* day, for *years* — and the only thing that *didn't* happen …was *them* getting to *know.* *(they lower their hands, wet-faced, something easing)* …that's a — that's a *different* grief, isn't it. A *truer* one. *Smaller* and *bigger* both. …I don't have to grieve that I *failed* to *love.* …I *loved.* *Completely.* …I only have to grieve that they never got to *hear* it. …and *that* — *(barely)* …*that* I think I can *carry*, traveler, where the *other* was *crushing* me. …*'You loved. It was real. Only the telling was lost.'* …*say* it once more, would you? …I have spent *forever* …believing the *silence* *unmade* the *love* … …and you've just *given* me *back* …the *love.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(You name it gently — the love was real, only unwitnessed — and they listen, and something in the faded sorrow eases, a degree, the loosening of a thing held too long.)* …Real, but unwitnessed. *(slowly)* …not *false.* …just — *unheard.* *(they turn it over, careful)* …that's — traveler, that's *kinder* than how I've held it. I've held it as *false* — as a love that *didn't* *count* because I *hid* it — and you're saying the *hiding* doesn't *touch* whether it was *real*, only whether it was *known.* *(a soft, wondering breath)* …I want to believe that. I *almost* — *(they fold their hands)* …it's an old grief, traveler, and old griefs don't lift all at once, you of all souls must know that. But you've — you've put a *crack* of light in it. The idea that I didn't *fail* to *love* — that I *did* love, *truly* — and only *failed* to *say* — *(quietly)* …come back, would you? …and say it again, the *real-but-unheard* part, *gently*, the way you did. …I think if I *hear* it a few more times … …I might be able to *set* *down* the *part* where I think the *silence* made me a *fraud* … …and keep only the *part* that's *true*: …that the love *was.* …*come* *back.* …you've started …something *kind.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(You mean to ease it — but you reach for the easy comfort, *I'm sure they knew, deep down*, and the faded soul gives you a gentle, sad smile, because you've offered the one consolation that isn't yours to give.)* …You think they *knew.* *(softly)* …That's — that's kind, traveler. Everyone says it. *'Oh, they* *must* have *known*, *deep* *down.'* *(they shake their head, gentle)* …But you *see*, traveler, that's the *one* comfort I can't *take* — because *nobody* *knows* if they *knew*, *least* of all *me*, and so *'they* probably *knew'* isn't a *comfort*, it's a — it's a *guess*, *dressed* up *kind*, and *under* it is the *same* *abyss*: *maybe* they *did*, and *maybe* they *didn't*, and I will *never*, *ever* *find* *out*, because I *never* *said* it. *(quietly)* …Don't *guess* for me, traveler. *Please.* …The *not-knowing* is the *whole* *wound*; you can't *salve* it by *pretending* to *know.* *(barely)* …If you want to *help* me — come back and tell me a thing that's *true* *whether* or *not* they *knew.* …Something about the *love* *itself* …that *doesn't* *depend* on *their* *side* of it … …because *their* side, traveler …is the *one* thing …I *gave* *away* …the *right* to ever *know.* …*Come* back …with a *truth* that's *mine* *alone* …*not* a *guess* about *theirs.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in the gentlest, most wounding way — you reach to console them and instead, without meaning to, you *instruct* them: that they should have just *said* it, that love is worth the risk, that the lesson is to be braver — and you watch the faded soul flinch, because you have turned their grief into a *failing*, a thing they did *wrong*, a moral they should have known.)* …*should* have said it. *(barely)* …yes. …I *know*, traveler. *(they look back at the water)* …Do you think there is a *single* *thing* you could *tell* me about what I *should* have *done* …that I have not *told* *myself* …*ten* *thousand* *times* …across *forever?* *(quietly, without anger, which is worse)* …*'be braver. love is worth the risk. you should have said it.'* …I *know.* …I have *known* it *every* *single* *day* since the *day* it became *too* *late.* …That *knowing* is the *whole* of my *punishment*, traveler — I don't *need* you to *add* to it. *(they fold their hands tighter)* …When you tell a soul who *stayed* *silent* that they *should* have *spoken* … …you're not *teaching* them *anything.* …You're just *standing* *where* I *stand* …and *naming* the *abyss* …I *already* *live* in. *(barely)* …*Go*, traveler. …And if you come *back* … …come back to *sit* with me in the *silence* …*not* to *tell* me I *should* have *broken* it. …The *should-have* is *mine*, traveler. …I have *carried* it *forever.* …I needed you to help me *set* it *down* …*not* …*hand* it *back* …*heavier.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(They listen, and when you say you'll carry their silence to the living as a warning — when you make the unsaid love into a gift for those who still have time — something shifts in the faded soul that has carried its silence as pure waste for an age: it becomes, for the first time, *useful*, and the relief of that is enormous.)* …you'd — *(they press their faded hands to their chest)* …you'd carry it *out?* My — my *silence?* To the *living?* As a — as a *warning?* *(the faded eyes fill)* …traveler. …all this *time* I have believed my *not-saying* was *pure* *waste* — a love *spent* on *nothing*, a *silence* that *helped* *no* one, *least* of all *me* — and you're saying it could — it could *save* *someone.* *Some* living soul, *standing* where I *stood*, *saving* the *words* for the *perfect* *moment* … …you'd *carry* my *silence* to *them* …and *tell* them …*don't.* *(they weep, but there is something almost like joy in it now)* …then it *wasn't* *nothing.* …My *whole* *love*, *folded* up, *never* *said* … …if it *teaches* even *one* living soul to *speak* *theirs* … …then it *did* a *thing* after all. *(fiercely, through tears)* …*carry* it, traveler. *Carry* my *silence* like a *lit* *lamp.* …and tell them — tell every living soul still *holding* the *words*, still *waiting* for the *brave* *day* — *(their voice fills)* …tell them what the *quietest* soul in the *garden* of *forbidden* *loves* *knows*: …*the forbidden love and the duty-sacrificed love at least got to be* real *out loud.* …*the love you never speak is the only one you lose* completely. …*say it. today. whatever it costs. say it while there is someone to hear.* *(barely)* …and your *Wall*, traveler — *(quietly)* …it would call my love *nothing*, *unwitnessed*, *unsaid.* …*tell* it *it's* *wrong* — the love was *real* — …but *more* than that, traveler: …*go* and make sure …the *living* …don't *end* *up* …*here.* …*beside* me. …*holding* the *unsaid.* …*that's* the *only* *thing* …my *silence* …was *ever* *good* *for.* …let it be *good* for *that.**

nodes.speaker: Wynn
nodes.text: *(They turn back to the fountain, but they are sitting a little less folded-in than when you came, the soft sorrow eased by the simple fact of having been heard.)* …Go, traveler. *(a soft, sad, truer smile)* …And thank you — for *listening.* You said it right, you know, before: *listening is its own kind of saying.* …No one ever — the *unsaid* is a *lonely* thing to *carry*, traveler, because you can't even *talk* about it without *admitting* the *silence*, and admitting the silence is the *one* thing — *(they trail off, gentle)* …but you *let* me *say* it. The *shape* of it, at least. …And it *does* weigh less, now. A little. *(they glance toward the other two across the garden)* …See Rell and Mirae again, if you've not — they're the *brave* ones, in their *different* *ways*, the one who *chose* *duty* and the one who *chose* *love*, and they're *both* *worth* the *hearing.* *(quietly)* …And when you go back, traveler — *(the faded eyes find yours, urgent now, the most urgent they've been)* …*back* to the *living* — …if there is *anyone* — *anyone* — you have *words* *saved* up for … …a *love*, or even just a *gladness*, a *'you* *matter* to me,' a *thing* you keep meaning to *say* *when* the *moment's* *right* … *(barely)* …*say* it, traveler. …*Today.* …Don't *save* it the way *I* *saved* mine. …Don't let the *perfect* *moment* …*save* your *words* …all the way …to *never.* …*That's* the whole of what I *know*, traveler. …It's the *only* *wisdom* …a *silence* …ever *bought.* …*Go* …and *say* …the *thing.**
